steps.title: Identify the sentence's only main verb
steps.content:
The sentence already has one main verb: "changed" ("Most of the original codes … later changed in response to …"). The blank must describe what happened to the codes beforehand without adding a second main verb, which would create a run-on or require a new clause boundary.
  • Choice A — ‘were assigned’: A finite past-passive verb; inserting it gives the sentence two main verbs with no conjunction or punctuation separating them — a run-on error. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘had been assigned’: A finite past-perfect verb; same problem as A — two main verbs, no legal boundary. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘assigned’: A past participle functioning as a modifier ("codes assigned by the committee"), keeping "changed" as the sentence's sole main verb. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘would be assigned’: A finite conditional verb; again produces two main verbs without a proper separator. ✗

steps.title: Confirm by reading the complete sentence with Choice C
steps.content: "Most of the original two-or three-digit codes assigned by the International Telegraph and Telephone Consultative Committee later changed in response to geopolitical circumstances or telecommunications needs." The participial phrase "assigned by …" modifies "codes," and "changed" remains the one main verb. The sentence is grammatically complete and correct. That confirms C is correct.
